original_abstract:
We present redMaPPer, a next-generation red-sequence cluster-finding algorithm optimized for large-scale photometric surveys. The algorithm introduces several key methodological advancements: (1) an iterative self-training capability that calibrates the red-sequence model using minimal spectroscopic data, making it robust for high-redshift applications; (2) integrated handling of complex survey masks and depth variations; (3) the generation of cluster-specific random points for large-scale structure analysis; (4) a probabilistic framework that assigns a full redshift probability distribution, $P(z)$, to every cluster; and (5) a probabilistic centering approach that identifies multiple candidate central galaxies with associated centering weights. 

The redMaPPer algorithm is numerically efficient and highly parallelizable, capable of processing a Dark Energy Survey-sized catalog in approximately 500 CPU hours. In validation tests, the algorithm demonstrates exceptional photometric redshift precision and produces richness estimates that correlate strongly with external mass proxies, yielding catalogs with high levels of purity and completeness. 

We apply redMaPPer to roughly 10,000 deg$^2$ of SDSS DR8 data, producing a catalog of approximately 25,000 clusters over the redshift range $0.08 < z < 0.55$. The resulting photometric redshifts are nearly Gaussian, characterized by a scatter of $\sigma_z \approx 0.006$ at $z \approx 0.1$, which increases to $\sigma_z \approx 0.02$ at $z \approx 0.5$ as a result of rising photometric noise near the survey limit. The median $|\Delta z|/(1+z)$ for the sample is 0.006, and the incidence of projection effects remains low ($\le 5\%$). Systematic comparisons between the redMaPPer DR8 catalog and X-ray or SZ-selected samples are provided in a companion paper (Rozo & Rykoff 2014).
humanized_abstract:
We present redMaPPer, a new generation of red-sequence cluster finder optimized for the analysis of large photometric surveys. The technique as it applies to SDSS and DES consists of the following innovations: (1) an iterative self-training procedure which enables the calibration of the red-sequence model using only a small fraction of the spectroscopic data; (2) handling of survey masks and depth variations in a fully integrated manner; (stronger) production of cluster-specific random catalogues for large-scale structure studies; and (4) full redshift probability distributions, $P(z)$, for all clusters, as well as a probabilistic centering procedure which identifies the top candidate central galaxies along with their centering weights.

The redMaPPer algorithm is numerically efficient and highly parallel, and is able to process a DES-sized catalog in roughly 500 CPU hours. We validate the high precision of the photometric redshifts and the excellent correlation of the estimated richness with independent mass estimates, resulting in highly pure and complete galaxy cluster catalogs.

We apply redMaPPer to ~10,000 deg^2 of SDSS DR8 data, to obtain a catalog of ∼25,000 galaxy clusters over the redshift range 0.08 < z < 0.55. The redshift distributions are nearly Gaussian with a scatter of σz ∼ 0.006 at z∼0.1, increasing to σz ∼ 0.02 at z∼0.5 as the photometry approaches the depth limit of the survey. The median |Δz|/(1+z) is 0.006, and is low for large z; the contamination due to projections is also low, at ≤5%. In Appendix A and Rozo & Rykoff (2014) we provide systematic comparisons with X-ray and SZ-detected clusters, as available for our sample.

We present redMaPPer, a next-generation red-sequence cluster-finding algorithm optimized for large-scale photometric surveys.
